comments.title: Notation inconsistency for input embeddings vs. Q/K/V matrices
comments.quote: We compute the query, key, and value vectors by multiplying the input embeddings Q, K, V ∈ R[N][×][d] with the parameterized matrices W[Q] ∈ R[d][×][n] , W[K] ∈ R[d][×][n] , and W[V] ∈ R[d][×][m] respectively
comments.explanation: At first glance, this sentence seems to describe standard attention mechanics, but the notation is internally contradictory. The authors label the input embeddings as Q, K, V and assign them dimension R^{N x d}. They then state these are multiplied by weight matrices to *compute* the query, key, and value vectors. In standard self-attention literature, X denotes the input embeddings (R^{N x d}), while Q, K, V denote the projected vectors, which would have dimensions R^{N x n} and R^{N x m} based on their own weight matrix definitions. Using Q, K, V for the raw inputs conflates the pre- and post-projection states. I checked Theorem 1 and the rest of the paper, and this ambiguous labeling persists. While the authors' intent is clear, the notation contradicts both standard conventions and the dimensional arithmetic they present. It should be corrected to use X for inputs or explicitly denote projected vectors to align dimensions properly.

comments.title: Inconsistency between reward metric and pruning objective description
comments.quote: "The reward of AUBER is the change in accuracy... evaluated on the mini-dev set" (Section 3.2) vs. "outputs the index of an attention head that is expected to increase the training accuracy when removed" (Section 3.4)
comments.explanation: The RL framework explicitly defines the reward as the change in accuracy on the mini-dev (validation) set to guide pruning while preventing overfitting. However, Section 3.4 states that the agent looks for heads that increase 'training accuracy'. While these metrics often correlate, they are distinct optimization targets, especially in a regularization context where the training data is explicitly partitioned for RL evaluation. A careful reader might assume the agent learns a policy optimized for train performance, which contradicts the formally defined validation-based reward signal. I initially wondered if this was a casual phrasing, but in RL papers, the distinction between train and eval rewards is critical for reproducibility. The description in Section 3.4 should be updated to reference validation accuracy to maintain consistency with the reward definition.

comments.title: Underspecified termination condition for layer-wise pruning
comments.quote:
if Bt[∗][.one] ~~h~~ ead left then
action ← H + 1
comments.explanation: Algorithm 1 (Line 8) forces the pruning loop to terminate when only one head remains in a layer, effectively preventing the agent from pruning the final head. I initially assumed this was an implicit architectural constraint, but the text does not explicitly justify why pruning must stop at one head. A reader might wonder if the agent could theoretically prune all heads in a layer and rely on residual connections, or if the authors simply want to preserve at least one head per layer for stability. Explicitly stating that each layer is constrained to retain a minimum of one attention head as a hard design choice would clarify the boundary condition and improve reproducibility, rather than leaving it as an unexplained loop termination.

comments.title: Notation clash for input embeddings in Section 2.1
comments.quote: We compute the query, key, and value vectors by multiplying the input embeddings Q, K, V ∈ R[N][×][d] with the parameterized matrices W[Q] ∈ R[d][×][n] , W[K] ∈ R[d][×][n] , and W[V] ∈ R[d][×][m] respectively
comments.explanation: Initially, the phrasing 'multiplying the input embeddings Q, K, V' raised a red flag because Q, K, and V are standard symbols for the *results* of the projections in self-attention. Checking the notation summary confirms that Q, K, V are defined as the resulting query, key, and value vectors, while the summary does not define a symbol for the input embeddings (typically X). The context does not resolve this: the text explicitly labels the input as Q, K, V, then says it computes Q, K, V by multiplying these inputs. This creates a circular notation where the same symbols denote both the pre-projection inputs and the post-projection outputs, which would confuse a reader tracking matrix dimensions or reproducing the forward pass.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of constant C in Theorem 1
comments.quote: Then, ∥Oi∥1 ≤ C∥Wi[V][∥][1][where the constant][ C][=][ N][∥][V][ ∥][1][.]
comments.explanation: The theorem attempts to bound the L1 norm of the attention head output by the L1 norm of its value weight matrix. The constant is given as C = N‖V‖₁. While I assumed V might refer to the input value matrix (as introduced earlier in the same sentence), this definition is problematic. If V is the input value matrix, its L1 norm scales with both sequence length N and embedding dimension d, making the constant highly variable and dependent on data distributions rather than model architecture. The bound also ignores the norms of the query and key matrices or the softmax attention weights, which are critical in standard attention analysis. Without clarification on whether V refers to a fixed baseline, a norm of normalized embeddings, or a typo, the theoretical justification for using ‖Wᵛᵢ‖₁ as a stability proxy remains unconvincing and mathematically underspecified.

comments.title: Imprecise phrasing of search space complexity
comments.quote: Naively designing actions would lead to 2[144] possible actions which are prohibitively large.
comments.explanation: The authors state that naive action design leads to 2¹⁴⁴ possible actions. Reading this carefully, I recognized that 2¹⁴⁴ actually represents the total number of possible pruning configurations (the combinatorial space of subsets of 144 heads), not the action space per step. The actual action space per decision is defined immediately before and after as H+1 (prune head 1..H or stop). The context suggests the authors meant to emphasize the exponential blowup of brute-force combinatorial search, but conflating configuration space size with 'actions' mischaracterizes the MDP formulation. A reader might incorrectly infer that the branching factor itself is exponential, or misunderstand how the dually-greedy approach structurally reduces the problem. The intent is understandable, but the terminology could mislead reproductions or theoretical comparisons.

comments.title: Non-standard notation for input matrices Q, K, V
comments.quote: Let Q, K, V ∈ R[N][×][d] be the input query, key, and value matrices
comments.explanation: Initially, this phrasing alarmed me as it directly contradicts standard self-attention notation, where the raw inputs are typically denoted X, and Q, K, V are the *projected* outputs (i.e., XW^Q, XW^K, XW^V). Checking Section 2.1 confirmed that the authors deliberately define the raw inputs as Q, K, V and state they are subsequently multiplied by W^Q, W^K, W^V. While the authors are internally consistent, this reversal of field conventions is highly atypical and likely to confuse readers familiar with standard transformer architectures. A reproducer might incorrectly assume Q, K, V refer to the projected spaces in later equations. The authors correctly identify the dimensions and projection matrices, but the notation choice creates an unnecessary barrier to comprehension. Severity: minor

comments.title: Undefined norm convention in Theorem 1
comments.quote: Then, ∥Oi∥1 ≤ C∥Wi[V][∥][1][where the constant][ C][=][ N][∥][V][ ∥][1][.]
comments.explanation: I initially worried that the bound might be dimensionally or mathematically invalid, but checking the derivation logic showed that if ∥·∥1 denotes the induced matrix 1-norm (maximum absolute column sum), the inequality holds: the softmax attention matrix is row-stochastic, so its induced 1-norm is bounded by N, yielding C = N∥V∥1. However, the theorem never specifies which norm convention is being used. If a reader interprets ∥·∥1 as the Frobenius norm, the bound would scale with √N instead of N. If interpreted as an element-wise L1 sum, the factorization into ∥V∥1∥W_i^V∥1 is not generally valid without additional assumptions. The authors correctly identify the dependency on sequence length N and the value matrix, but omitting the norm type leaves the theorem ambiguous and difficult to verify or extend rigorously. Severity: moderate

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of head output O_i
comments.quote: Let Oi be the output of the i[th] head.
comments.explanation: At first, I wondered how O_i could be bounded solely by ∥W_i^V∥1 without involving the output projection matrix W_i^O. In standard multi-head attention, a single head's output is conventionally defined as A_i V_i W_i^O (before concatenation). If O_i includes W_i^O, the bound should logically incorporate ∥W_i^O∥1 as well. The omission strongly suggests that the authors define O_i as the attention-weighted value matrix A_i V_i (i.e., pre-output-projection), which aligns with their focus on how V_i scales the output. The authors correctly capture the core mechanism that head importance is driven by V_i, but failing to explicitly state whether O_i is pre- or post-projection creates ambiguity. A careful reader might misapply the bound to the final projected head output. Severity: minor

comments.title: Omission of attention scaling factor in constant C
comments.quote: [where the constant][ C][=][ N][∥][V][ ∥][1][.]
comments.explanation: My initial concern was whether the constant N overlooked a standard architectural detail. Standard scaled dot-product attention divides the dot products by √n (where n is the query/key dimension) before applying softmax. This scaling factor reduces the variance of attention scores and consequently tightens the bound on the output norm. The provided constant C = N∥V∥1 ignores the 1/√n factor. While the bound remains mathematically valid (and slightly looser) without it, formally omitting the scaling factor glosses over a fundamental step in modern transformer attention mechanisms. To be precise, the authors should either state C = (N/√n)∥V∥1 or explicitly note that the scaling is excluded for a looser but simpler bound. Severity: minor

comments.title: Missing negative sign in exponential decay formula
comments.quote: ϵ = ϵfinal +[ϵ][initial][ −][ϵ][f][inal] (3) e[s/ϵ][decay]
comments.explanation: The text explicitly describes a 'decaying-epsilon-greedy manner' and states that the exploration rate 'decreases exponentially'. However, Equation (3) is written with a positive exponent $e^{s/\epsilon_{decay}}$. Since $s$ (total actions taken) is non-negative, the exponential term would grow without bound, causing $\epsilon$ to increase rather than decay. The authors' intent and standard RL practice require a negative sign in the exponent ($e^{-s/\epsilon_{decay}}$). This omission fundamentally misrepresents the mathematical mechanism governing exploration schedule decay.

comments.title: Numerical discrepancy in reported performance improvement
comments.quote: AUBER improves the performance of BERT by up to 9.39% on those datasets.
comments.explanation: The authors claim an improvement of 'up to 9.39%' on the MRPC and CoLA datasets. Checking the reported means in Table 2: MRPC improves from 84.07 to 85.62 (+1.55 percentage points), and CoLA improves from 57.01 to 60.59 (+3.58 percentage points). Neither value matches 9.39%. If the claim accidentally refers to WNLI, the absolute improvement is 56.06 - 46.48 = 9.58 percentage points, which still does not match. If referring to relative improvement, the WNLI relative gain is ~20.6%. The 9.39% figure is inconsistent with the provided table data and is repeated verbatim in both the Abstract and Conclusion, suggesting a persistent typographical or calculation error.

comments.title: Overstatement of empirical dominance in ablation study
comments.quote: Note that AUBER, which uses the value matrix to obtain state vectors, outperforms AUBER-Query and AUBER-Key on all four tasks.
comments.explanation: In Section 4.3.1, the authors assert that AUBER outperforms the query and key variants on 'all four tasks'. Consulting Table 3 reveals that on the WNLI dataset, AUBER, AUBER-Query, and AUBER-Key all report identical accuracy (56.34). Thus, AUBER ties rather than outperforms the baselines on WNLI. The same imprecision appears in Section 4.3.2 where AUBER-L2 ties AUBER on RTE (66.43). While the overall conclusion of the ablation study remains valid, the textual summary is factually inaccurate and should be qualified to reflect the ties (e.g., 'outperforms... on three out of four tasks').

progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: The paper presents a clear motivation for regularizing BERT via attention head pruning and proposes a reinforcement learning (DQN) framework to automate the pruning process, which is a reasonable departure from heuristic-based methods. The abstract and introduction are well-written and effectively convey the problem and the proposed solution. However, several significant issues emerge from the limited excerpt. First, the claimed novelty is questionable: applying RL to prune neural network components (especially attention heads) has been explored in prior work (e.g., for model compression), and the paper does not adequately distinguish its approach from these existing efforts. Second, the theoretical justification (Theorem 1, mentioned but not shown) is critical for understanding why the L1 norm of the value matrix is a suitable state feature, yet it is absent from the provided text, weakening the paper's analytical rigor. Third, while the ablation study is promised, the lack of concrete evidence (e.g., baseline comparisons, performance variances) in the introduction makes the claimed 9.39% improvement seem unsubstantiated. Finally, the paper's framing as "regularization" rather than "compression" is somewhat misleading—pruning heads for performance gains on small datasets is a form of regularization, but the discussion of overfitting and the trade-off between pruning and fine-tuning is insufficiently detailed. Overall, the paper is clearly structured and easy to follow, but it must address these novelty, theoretical, and empirical gaps to be convincing.

comments.title: Theorem 1 constant C is not properly justified given provided definitions
comments.quote: Then, ∥Oi∥1 ≤ C∥WiV∥1 where the constant C = N∥V∥1. □
comments.explanation: The theorem claims C = N∥V∥1, but V is defined as an input matrix ∈ R^{N×d}. However, ∥V∥1 is a matrix norm (likely elementwise L1 norm), and the proof is referenced to Appendix A.1 (not provided). Without the full derivation, it is unclear whether C depends on V (which is input data) or on fixed parameters. If C depends on the input V, the bound is data-dependent, which may weaken the theoretical justification for using WiV's norm as a state representation. The authors should clarify whether this bound holds uniformly or only for specific inputs. This is a moderate concern because the claim is central to justifying the state representation. Additionally, calling C a 'constant' is misleading as it depends on the input V, and the L1 norm of V is not standardly defined (matrix L1 norm vs elementwise L1). The theorem does not establish that pruning a head with small L1 norm has small effect on output, because C depends on the input V and the query/key product.

comments.title: Algorithm 1 dual-stage use of DQN is underspecified
comments.quote: Stage 1: Train DQN for Layer i ... Stage 2: Prune Layer i ... use the trained DQN to make the final decision on which heads to prune.
comments.explanation: The algorithm description (Figure 2 and Algorithm 1) indicates training the DQN for E episodes, then using the trained policy network to prune ('Bt.prune(P.final_policy(l))'). However, it is not specified how the 'final_policy(l)' is derived from the trained network. Is it the greedy policy (always choose max Q-value) or does it involve a separate validation set? The line 'P.optimize(M)' and 'Bt.prune(P.final_policy(l))' leaves ambiguity about whether the final pruning uses the policy network with or without exploration. The pseudocode also omits key DQN details: no mention of target network, how ε is tracked/decayed globally vs per layer, and the update frequency for the target network. These omissions could hinder reproducibility.

comments.title: Under-specified epsilon decay rate formula
comments.quote: ϵ = ϵfinal +[ϵ][initial][ −][ϵ][f][inal] (3) e[s/ϵ][decay]
comments.explanation: The equation for epsilon-greedy decay is ambiguous due to OCR corruption. The intended formula likely ϵ = ϵ_final + (ϵ_initial − ϵ_final) * e^{−s/ϵ_decay}, but the current rendering shows e[s/ϵ][decay] which could be misinterpreted as e^{s/ϵ_decay} (growing over time) rather than the standard exponential decay. The prose says 'epsilon decreases exponentially with the decay rate ϵdecay of 256', which suggests e^{-s/256}. The error is primarily a formatting/OCR issue, but since a reader reproducing the method needs the exact formula, the ambiguity is a moderate reproducibility concern.

comments.title: Claimed improvement of 'up to 9.39%' inconsistent with reported table values
comments.quote: AUBER improves the performance of BERT by up to 9.39% on those datasets.
comments.explanation: The sentence states that AUBER improves performance by up to 9.39% on MRPC and CoLA (the datasets mentioned just before). However, Table 2 shows absolute improvements of 1.55% (85.62 vs 84.07) on MRPC and 3.58 (60.59 vs 57.01) on CoLA (using Matthew's correlation for CoLA). Even if the metric is accuracy for both, the improvements are far below 9.39% absolute. The 9.39% figure might be a relative improvement (e.g., error reduction) or might refer to WNLI (where the absolute gain is 9.58), but the text explicitly ties it to 'those datasets' (MRPC and CoLA). Without clarification, a careful reader would find a mismatch between the textual claim and the tabulated results, potentially undermining trust in the reported numbers.

comments.title: Ambiguous reward function phrasing for exit action
comments.quote: The reward for action a = H + 1 is a hyper-parameter that can be adjusted to encourage or discourage active pruning. In AUBER, it is set to 0 to encourage the DQN agent to prune only when the expected change in accuracy is positive.
comments.explanation: Setting the reward for the exit action to 0 seems to discourage exiting unless further pruning yields negative expected reward. However, the text says it 'encourage[s] the DQN agent to prune only when the expected change in accuracy is positive' — this is confusing: a reward of 0 for exit compared to a positive reward for a good pruning action incentives pruning when beneficial, but if all pruning actions yield negative reward, the agent would still exit (getting 0) rather than continuing (getting negative). This is logically consistent. However, the phrase 'can be adjusted to encourage or discourage active pruning' is vague — it does not specify how this hyperparameter interacts with the epsilon-greedy exploration or whether the value 0 is optimal. This minor ambiguity could be clarified.

comments.title: Stray sentence fragment disrupts flow

comments.explanation: This sentence appears abruptly before the 'Implementation' paragraph. It seems to be a remnant from an earlier draft or a misplaced line. The surrounding context does not integrate it; the next sentence begins a new paragraph about implementation. While the content is consistent with the method described earlier, the placement disrupts the flow and may confuse readers about whether it is part of the results discussion or a repeated note.

comments.title: Ambiguous claim: 'reward for action a = H + 1 is set to 0 to encourage the DQN agent to prune only when the expected change in accuracy is positive'
comments.quote: The reward for action a = H + 1 is a hyper-parameter that can be adjusted to encourage or discourage active pruning. In AUBER, it is set to 0 to encourage the DQN agent to prune only when the expected change in accuracy is positive.
comments.explanation: Setting the stop-action reward to 0 does not inherently encourage pruning only when accuracy improvement is positive; it only means the agent receives no reward for stopping. If the agent expects negative rewards from continuing (i.e., pruning further heads decreases accuracy), it may stop. However, if the agent expects zero or positive reward from additional pruning, it might continue even when stopping would be better. The statement is slightly oversimplified but not fundamentally wrong. The overall learning dynamics also depend on the Q-learning update and discount factor γ=1. This is a minor framing concern.

comments.title: Theorem 1's constant C = N ||V||_1 may have subscript or norm ambiguity
comments.quote: Then, ∥Oi∥1 ≤ C∥Wi[V]∥1 where the constant C = N∥V ∥1.
comments.explanation: The statement says C = N * ||V||_1, but V is the input value matrix (dimensions N x d). The L1 norm of V is not standardly defined: is it the matrix L1 norm (max column sum) or elementwise L1? Additionally, N appears twice (once as token count, once as part of C). The constant depends on ||V||_1 which itself changes with the input, making the bound data-dependent and potentially loose. The authors claim this 'justifies' using L1 norm of W_i^V as a state element, but the bound only shows that the output norm is proportional to ||W_i^V||_1, which is trivially true from linearity. The theorem does not establish that pruning a head with small L1 norm has small effect on output, because C depends on the input V and the query/key product (implicit in attention weights). This is a questionable justification — it oversimplifies the relationship. However, since the proof is in the appendix (not shown) and the claim is about 'importance', this is a moderate issue: the theoretical grounding is weaker than presented.

comments.title: Underspecified: 'fine-tuned with a smaller learning rate' — exact value unclear
comments.quote: After the best pruning policy for layer l of BERT is found, the BERT model pruned according to the best pruning policy is fine-tuned with a smaller learning rate.
comments.explanation: While the learning rate for fine-tuning after each layer is given as 0.000002 in Section 4.1, the phrase 'smaller learning rate' is vague. A reader might wonder if it refers to a fraction of the initial fine-tuning rate (which was 2e-5 or 1e-5). The numeric value is later provided, so this is minor — but the text could be clearer.

comments.title: Theorem 1 misuses 'constant' for an input-dependent quantity
comments.quote: Let Q, K, V ∈ R[N][×][d] be the input query, key, and value matrices, ... Then, ∥Oi∥1 ≤ C∥Wi[V][∥][1]where the constant C = N∥V ∥1.
comments.explanation: The theorem states that ∥Oi∥1 ≤ C∥Wi^V∥1 with C = N∥V∥1. Here ∥V∥1 is the L1 norm of the input value matrix V, which varies across different sentences or batches. Calling it a 'constant' is misleading because it is not independent of the input. A proper statement would note that the bound holds with a factor that depends on the input V, which is the same for all heads in a layer. The justification for using the L1 norm of Wi^V as an importance signal is weakened by this imprecision, though the relative ordering across heads remains meaningful because C is shared. The authors should clarify that C is not a global constant but a quantity that depends on the input V.

comments.title: Algorithm 1 omits the target network and ε-greedy details needed for DQN
comments.quote: Algorithm 1: ... Initialize policy network P and replay memory M ... action ← P.choose action(sl) ... P.optimize(M)
comments.explanation: The paper describes DQN with a target network and decaying ε-greedy exploration, but Algorithm 1 does not mention the target network at all, nor does it specify how ε is managed (e.g., whether it is reset per layer or accumulated globally). In line 11, 'P.choose_action(sl)' presumably uses ε-greedy, but the decay formula (Equation 3) depends on the total number of actions taken so far (s). The algorithm does not define how s is tracked or when the target network is updated. These omissions are significant for reproducibility; a reader implementing AUBER would need to infer these details from the text. The authors should either extend the pseudocode or explicitly refer to the standard DQN procedure from Section 2.3.

progressive__glm-4.7-flash.label: Progressive (glm-4.7-flash)
progressive__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: The paper introduces AUBER, a novel application of reinforcement learning to the problem of regularizing BERT via attention head pruning, demonstrating a clear improvement over heuristic-based approaches. The manuscript is well-written and provides a solid foundation for the proposed method, which uses Deep Q-Learning (DQN) to automatically determine the optimal layer-wise pruning strategy. However, a significant concern regarding the practicality of this work lies in the computational complexity implied by the methodology; specifically, the process of training DQN for each layer before finalizing the pruning decision suggests a non-trivial overhead that may be undesirable for the target use case (fine-tuning on small datasets). Additionally, while the theoretical justification for the state features is noted in the contributions, the paper must rigorously demonstrate that the learned RL policy generalizes effectively across downstream tasks rather than merely overfitting to the specific training set structure used to define the reward signal.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation for attention matrix dimensions in Section 2.1
comments.quote: We compute the query, key, and value vectors by multiplying the input embeddings $Q, K, V \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times d}$ with the parameterized matrices $W^{[Q]} \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times n}, W^{[K]} \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times n},$ and $W^{[V]} \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times m}$ respectively, where $N$ is the number of tokens in the sentence, and $n, m,$ and $d$ are query, value, and embedding dimension respectively.
comments.explanation: The authors define the input to the multiplication ($Q, K, V$) as being of dimension $d$, i.e., $Q, K, V \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times d}$. However, they also define the projection matrices as having output dimensions $n$ (for $Q, K$) and $m$ (for $V$), i.e., $W^{[Q]} \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times n}$ and $W^{[V]} \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times m}$. Mathematically, $Q, K, V$ would then have dimensions $N \times n$ and $N \times m$, respectively. Writing $Q \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times d}$ is inconsistent with this calculation unless $n=d$ (which contradicts the separate definitions of $n$ and $d$). This confusion could mislead readers trying to reconstruct the exact dimensions of the matrices involved in the attention mechanism.

comments.title: Implicit constraint in Algorithm 1 prevents pruning the last head
comments.quote: Algorithm 1: AUBER: Automatic BERT Regularization ... 8: if Bt[∗].one ~~h~~ ead left then 9: action ← H + 1 10: else 11: action ← P.choose action(sl) ... while action ∕= H + 1 do ...
comments.explanation: In line 8, the algorithm explicitly overwrites the agent's decision (`P.choose action`) if only one attention head remains (`one ~~h~~ ead left`). It forces the action to become `H + 1` (stop), thereby preventing the agent from pruning the final remaining head. While this ensures the model never becomes empty (preserving at least one head), the text and algorithm description do not explicitly justify this constraint. Readers might assume the agent has full control to prune the last head if the estimated reward dictates, making this hidden logic a point of ambiguity. Furthermore, forcing a termination in the 'terminal phase' (only one head left) interrupts the natural trajectory of the state-action loop.

comments.title: Numerical inconsistency between claimed improvement and reported data
comments.quote: AUBER improves the performance of BERT by up to 9.39% on those datasets.
comments.explanation: The sentence claims that AUBER achieves a 9.39% accuracy improvement on MRPC and CoLA. However, Table 2 in the context clearly states the improvements for these datasets are approximately 1.55% (MRPC: 85.62 vs 84.07) and 3.58% (CoLA: 60.59 vs 57.01), respectively. The figure 9.39% is notably close to the improvement observed on the WNLI dataset (approx. 9.58%), suggesting the authors likely mistakenly attributed the WNLI result to the sentence describing MRPC and CoLA. This inconsistency undermines the accuracy of the experimental results section.

The figure 9.39% is notably close to the improvement observed on the WNLI dataset (approx. 9.58%), suggesting the authors likely mistakenly attributed the WNLI result to the sentence describing MRPC and CoLA. This inconsistency undermines the accuracy of the experimental results section.

comments.title: OCR/Typo in competitor name
comments.quote: Confdence
comments.explanation: Table 2 lists a pruning competitor as 'Confdence'. While the text describes the method as 'smallest confidence score,' the table's abbreviation appears to be a typo or OCR error for 'Confidence'. This makes the table slightly difficult to read.

comments.title: Typographical error in conference venue name
comments.quote: In NuerIPS, 2019.
comments.explanation: The correct spelling for the annual 'Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems' is 'NeurIPS'. The passage lists 'NuerIPS', which appears to be a typographical error. Although any expert in the field would immediately recognize this as a mistake for the NeurIPS venue where the Michel et al. (2019) paper (which compares against AUBER) was presented, it is an error nonetheless. It is not a major issue as it does not fundamentally alter the scientific claims, but it is a technical flaw in the presentation of the reference.
